content:
A tiny click, a whole backend circus.

15 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You click a button.
That’s it.
Maybe it is Book Now, Pay, Add to Cart, Search, Send OTP, Follow, Upload, or the most dangerous button in software history -
“Confirm Payment.”
From your side, it feels like one innocent tap.
From the system’s side, that button click becomes a full journey through your phone, the internet, DNS, CDN, load balancers, API gateways, backend services, caches, databases, queues, search engines, payment gateways, notification systems, and monitoring tools.
Basically, you clicked once.
The backend started doing cardio.
This article is the map of that journey.
We will not go super deep into every single component here, because each one deserves its own article. Redis alone can become a whole emotional relationship. API Gateway, CDN, queues, Elasticsearch, database transactions, idempotency, locking - all of them are big topics.
But by the end of this article, you should have a clear mental model of what usually happens when a user clicks a button inside a big app.
Not just “frontend calls backend.”
That is like saying a movie is “people talking.”
Technically true. Uselessly incomplete.
First, the button does not directly hit the backend
Let’s say you click Place Order.
Before the request leaves your phone or laptop, the frontend already does some checks.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Arkan Perdana on UnsplashIt may ask -
Is the user logged in ?
Is the form valid ?
Is the button already clicked once ?
Is the item selected ?
Is the network available ?
Should we disable the button now ?
Should we show a loader ?
That is why many apps disable a button immediately after you click it.
It is not because the designer was bored.
It is because users double-click important buttons like they are playing Mortal Kombat.
For simple buttons like “Like Post,” duplicate clicks may not be too dangerous. But for buttons like Pay Now, Book, Transfer Money, or Submit Application, duplicate clicks can create serious bugs.
Imagine clicking Pay twice and getting charged twice.
Now the user is angry, support team is crying, and the backend engineer is pretending to be offline on Slack.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Uday Mittal on UnsplashSo for critical actions, the frontend or backend often uses something called an idempotency key.
Do not panic. Fancy word, simple idea.
An idempotency key is a unique identifier for a request.
It tells the backend
“If this exact operation comes again, do not repeat the dangerous part.”
So if the user clicks Pay twice, or the network retries the request, the backend can safely say -
“I have already processed this request. I will return the same result instead of charging again.”
This is very important in payments, order creation, booking systems, wallet transactions, coupon redemption, and anything where “doing it twice” is bad.
So the first lesson is
A button click is not just a click. It is an intention that must be protected.
The frontend creates an HTTP request
Once the frontend decides the click is valid, it creates an HTTP request.
Something like this -
POST /orders
Authorization: Bearer user_token
Content-Type: application/json
Idempotency-Key: req_12345
{
"itemId": "item_789",
"quantity": 1,
"addressId": "addr_456"
}This request has a few important parts.
The HTTP method tells what kind of action this is.
GET usually means - “Give me some data.”
POST usually means - “Create something or trigger an action.”
PUT or PATCH usually means - “Update something.”
DELETE means -“Remove something, hopefully the correct thing.”
The path tells which resource or action the request is about.
For example
/search
/orders
/payments
/users/meThe headers carry metadata.
Headers may include authentication token, request ID, content type, app version, device info, language, trace ID, and idempotency key.
The body carries the actual input.
For example, item ID, address ID, quantity, search query, payment method, or selected seat.
Now this request needs to reach the correct backend.
But the internet does not understand your cute domain name directly.
It needs an address.
That is where DNS comes in.
DNS: finding where the app lives
Humans remember names.
Computers prefer numbers.
You understand
api.mybigapp.comBut the internet needs an IP address.
So your device asks DNS
“Where is api.mybigapp.com ?”
DNS replies with an IP address, or sometimes an address that points to a CDN, load balancer, or edge network.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Think of DNS like asking
“Bro, where does this backend live ?”
DNS says
“Go here.”
Without DNS, you would need to remember IP addresses for every app.
Imagine saying
“Hey, check out my startup at 13.227.74.129.”
Very investable. Very romantic.
DNS results can be cached at many levels - browser, operating system, router, ISP, DNS resolver. So every request does not always perform a fresh DNS lookup.
Once the app knows where to go, it opens a secure connection using HTTPS.
HTTPS uses encryption so that people sitting between you and the server cannot casually read your request.
This matters because requests may contain tokens, personal data, payment intent, location, or other sensitive information.
Now the request is ready to move.
CDN: sometimes the backend does not need to wake up
Before the request reaches your main backend, it may hit a CDN.
CDN means Content Delivery Network.
A CDN stores content closer to users.
For example, if your backend is in Mumbai and a user is in London, the user should not always fetch images, JavaScript files, videos, and static assets all the way from Mumbai.
That would be slow.
So CDN providers keep copies of cacheable content at edge locations around the world.
This is useful for
Images
Videos
CSS files
JavaScript bundles
Fonts
Public static pages
Product/event thumbnails
Landing pages
Documentation pages
So when a user opens an app, many things may come from the CDN before the backend is even involved.
But here is the important part -
CDN is great for things that are public and cacheable.
CDN is dangerous for things that are private, personalized, or constantly changing.
Good CDN usage
Show product image
Load app JavaScript
Load public landing pageBad CDN usage
Show user bank balance
Show private order details
Show latest wallet amount
Confirm payment statusThe CDN is like a smart snack counter near your house.
It can quickly give common items.
But you do not ask it to sign legal documents.
For button clicks like POST /orders or POST /payments, the CDN usually does not process the action. It forwards the request to the origin/backend.
For read-heavy actions like loading public pages or images, the CDN may serve the response directly.
So CDN helps reduce latency and protects your servers from unnecessary traffic.
But it is not a replacement for backend logic.
Load balancer: choosing which server gets the request
Behind a big app, there is usually not one backend server.
There may be dozens, hundreds, or thousands of backend instances.
Why ?
Because one server cannot handle everything forever.
Also, servers fail.
They are just computers with responsibilities.
A load balancer sits in front of multiple servers and distributes traffic among them.
Example
Request 1 -> Server A
Request 2 -> Server B
Request 3 -> Server CIt also checks which servers are healthy.
If Server B is down, the load balancer stops sending traffic to it.
This allows horizontal scaling.
Instead of buying one giant machine and praying to the cloud gods, you run many machines and distribute traffic.
Load balancers can work at different levels.
At a lower level, they may distribute TCP connections.
At a higher level, they may understand HTTP paths and route requests based on URL.
For example
/search -> Search servers
/orders -> Order servers
/payments -> Payment serversThis starts sounding similar to an API Gateway, but they are not exactly the same.
Simple mental model
Load balancer distributes traffic.
API Gateway controls API behavior.
Load balancer asks
“Which healthy server should handle this ?”
API Gateway asks
“Is this request allowed, authenticated, rate-limited, logged, and routed to the right service ?”
Both may exist together.
API Gateway: the bouncer of the backend club
Now the request may reach the API Gateway.
The API Gateway is the front door of your backend system.
Actually, it is more than a front door.
It is the receptionist, security guard, traffic cop, CCTV camera, and sometimes the angry bouncer who says -
“You made 500 requests in 2 seconds. Go drink water.”
An API Gateway can handle
Authentication
Authorization
Rate limiting
Request validation
Routing
Logging
API versioning
Header enrichment
Request transformation
Response transformation
Blocking suspicious traffic
For example, when the request reaches the gateway, it may check -
Is the token valid ?
Is this user allowed to call this API ?
Is the app version supported ?
Is the request body valid ?
Has this user crossed the rate limit ?
Is this IP suspicious ?
Which backend service should handle this request ?
If the request is bad, the gateway may reject it before it reaches internal services.
This is useful because backend services should not individually fight every garbage request from the internet.
But one important warning
API Gateway should not become the brain of your business logic.
It should not decide complex rules like
Can this user buy this item ?
Is this coupon valid for this order ?
Should this payment be refunded ?
Can this inventory be reserved ?
That belongs inside backend services.
Gateway protects and routes.
Services think.
Backend service: where business logic happens
Now the request reaches the actual backend service.
Let’s say it is an Order Service.
The service may now perform business checks.
For example
Does the user exist ?
Is the item valid ?
Is the quantity available ?
Is the address serviceable ?
Is the price still correct ?
Is the coupon valid ?
Is this request duplicated ?
Can this user perform this action ?
A weak backend service is just
Take request -> insert into database -> return successA better backend service is
Validate request
Check user
Check item
Check inventory
Calculate final price
Check duplicate request
Create order
Reserve inventory
Start payment
Publish event
Return responseBackend services contain business rules.
This is where big apps stop being simple CRUD apps.
A button like Search may mostly read data.
A button like Place Order changes data.
A button like Pay Now may involve external systems.
A button like Book Slot may require strong consistency.
A button like Follow may update social graph, feed ranking, notifications, and counters.
Different buttons have different seriousness levels.
You cannot treat “load homepage” and “transfer ₹1,00,000” the same way.
Unless you enjoy production incidents.
Cache: do we already know the answer ?
Before hitting the database, the backend service may check a cache.
A cache is a fast storage layer used to avoid repeating expensive work.
The most common example is Redis.
Redis is often used as
Cache
Session store
Rate limiter
Temporary lock store
Counter store
Leaderboard store
OTP attempt tracker
Short-lived reservation store
Pub/Sub system
Queue-like system in some cases
In this article, we will only use Redis as part of the map. Later, Redis deserves its own deep dive because calling Redis “just a cache” is like calling Virat Kohli “just a guy with a bat.”
A common caching pattern is cache-aside.
Flow
Backend checks cache
If data exists, return it
If not, fetch from database
Store result in cache
Return responseExample
Get item details
-> Check Redis
-> Cache miss
-> Read from database
-> Store in Redis for 5 minutes
-> Return item detailsThis reduces database load and improves latency.
